child_sections.title: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: This report by the Ethiopian Ministry of Finance and IFS TaxDev team analyzes import tax expenditures for fiscal years 2018/19 through 2020/21. It provides estimates and policy recommendations for customs duty, excise duty, VAT, and surtax. The project was funded by UK aid to support tax analysis capabilities.

child_sections.title: 5 concludes and offers recommendations.
child_sections.summary: A 3% withholding tax is levied on the CIF value of imports, serving as a credit against future income tax liabilities rather than a final tax. Consequently, this report excludes import withholding tax from its analysis.

child_sections.title: 2    For details, see Appendix D.
child_sections.summary: The Ethiopian government uses import tax incentives to stimulate investment and economic development. However, transparency is limited because beneficiaries often fail to file returns, hindering revenue tracking. This reporting gap complicates monitoring and creates potential opportunities for corruption.

child_sections.title: 3.1  Defining the benchmark tax system
child_sections.summary: This report defines tax expenditures as deviations from a benchmark tax system, excluding international agreements and diplomat exemptions while focusing on positive import tax expenditures. Estimates utilize the revenue forgone method, which assumes no behavioral changes but acknowledges limitations regarding potential overestimation. The analysis further notes that import tax expenditures may indirectly lead to higher collections of domestic taxes.

child_sections.title: 3.2  Estimating tax expenditures
child_sections.summary: This report defines tax expenditure as the difference between benchmark and actual Ethiopian import taxes. Since taxes are charged sequentially, early exemptions reduce the tax base for subsequent levies. Therefore, indirect revenue losses are attributed to the origin tax rather than the downstream tax.

child_sections.title: 3.3  Data
child_sections.summary: This report analyzes merged Ethiopian customs microdata from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21 covering import values and taxes. A system transition likely caused duplicate records, potentially overestimating tax expenditures by billions in the first two fiscal years. Consequently, interpreting financial trends requires caution regarding these data limitations.

child_sections.title: 4.1  Overall tax expenditures
child_sections.summary: In FY 2020/21, total tax expenditures surged 54% to ETB 120.7 billion. This growth significantly outpaced the 18% increase in total import values. Consequently, expenditures represented 21.3% of imports and 2.8% of GDP, both higher than the previous year.

child_sections.title: E
child_sections.summary: This Ethiopian Ministry of Finance report estimates import tax expenditures reached up to 3.7% of GDP between FY 2018/19 and 2020/21, though data reliability was impacted by IT system transitions. Second schedule privileges decreased significantly due to policy removals. Capital goods exemptions grew because higher vehicle excise tax rates raised their value.
The text establishes ECC standard rates as the primary benchmark. These rates serve as a foundational reference point for evaluation.

child_sections.title: 4.2  Customs duty
child_sections.summary: Customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopia surged 67% to ETB 38.9 billion in FY 2020/21. Exemptions drove this increase, comprising 71% of the total and growing steadily over the past three years. These expenditures represented 0.9% of GDP and 32.2% of all import tax expenditures for the fiscal year.

child_sections.title: 4.3  Excise duty
child_sections.summary: Ethiopia's excise duty tax expenditures surged 117% to ETB 18.8 billion in FY 2020/21, driven primarily by unpaid vehicle import liabilities. Direct effects comprised over 84% of this total, while indirect effects through VAT and surtax tripled compared to prior years. These expenditures ultimately represented 0.4% of GDP and 15.6% of all import tax expenditures for the period.

Ethiopian excise tax expenditures shifted dramatically between FY 2018/19 and 2020/21, with vehicle imports surging to 68.7% of the total. Conversely, shares for mineral oil and prepared foods declined sharply, reflecting major fluctuations in commodity-based tax expenditures reported by the Ministry of Finance.
